title: Pictor Ignotus
content:
В В В В I could have painted pictures like that youthпїЅs
В В В В Ye praise so. How my soul springs up! No bar
В В В В Stayed me, ah, thought which saddens while it soothes!
В В В В Never did fate forbid me, star by star,
В В В В To outburst on your night, with all my gift
В В В В Of fires from God: nor would my flesh have shrunk
В В В В From seconding my soul, with eyes uplift
В В В В And wide to heaven, or, straight like thunder, sunk
В В В В To the centre, of an instant; or around
В В В В Turned calmly and inquisitive, to scan
В В В В The license and the limit, space and bound,
В В В В Allowed to Truth made visible in man.
В В В В And, like that youth ye praise so, all I saw,
В В В В Over the canvas could my hand have flung,
В В В В Each face obedient to its passionпїЅs law,
В В В В Each passion clear proclaimed without a tongue:
В В В В Whether Hope rose at once in all the blood,
В В В В A tip-toe for the blessing of embrace,
В В В В Or Rapture drooped the eyes, as when her brood
В В В В Pull down the nesting doveпїЅs heart to its place;
В В В В Or Confidence lit swift the forehead up,
В В В В And locked the mouth fast, like a castle braved,
В В В В O human faces! hath it spilt, my cup?
В В В В What did ye give me that I have not saved?
В В В В Nor will I say I have not dreamed (how well!)
В В В В Of going, I, in each new picture, forth,
В В В В As, making new hearts beat and bosoms swell,
В В В В To Pope or Kaiser, East, West, South, or North,
В В В В Bound for the calmly satisfied great State,
В В В В Or glad aspiring little burgh, it went,
В В В В Flowers cast upon the car which bore the freight,
В В В В Through old streets named afresh from the event,
В В В В Till it reached home, where learned Age should greet
В В В В My face, and Youth, the star not yet distinct
В В В В Above his hair, lie learning at my feet!
В В В В Oh, thus to live, I and my picture, linked
В В В В With love about, and praise, till life should end,
В В В В And then not go to Heaven, but linger here,
В В В В Here on my earth, earthпїЅs every man my friend,
В В В В The thought grew frightful, пїЅtwas so wildly dear!
В В В В But a voice changed it. Glimpses of such sights
В В В В Have scared me, like the revels through a door
В В В В Of some strange house of idols at its rites!
В В В В This world seemed not the world it was, before:
В В В В Mixed with my loving trusting ones, there trooped
В В В В . . . Who summoned those cold faces that begun
В В В В To press on me and judge me? Though I stooped
В В В В Shrinking, as from the soldiery a nun,
В В В В They drew me forth, and spite of me . . . enough!
В В В В These buy and sell our pictures, take and give,
В В В В Count them for garniture and household-stuff,
В В В В And where they live needs must our pictures live
В В В В And see their faces, listen to their prate,
В В В В Partakers of their daily pettiness,
В В В В Discussed of, пїЅThis I love, or this I hate,
В В В В This likes me more, and this affects me less!пїЅ
В В В В Wherefore I chose my portion. If at whiles
В В В В My heart sinks, as monotonous I paint
В В В В These endless cloisters and eternal aisles
В В В В With the same series, Virgin, Babe, and Saint,
В В В В With the same cold calm beautiful regard,
В В В В At least no merchant traffics in my heart;
В В В В The sanctuaryпїЅs gloom at least shall ward
В В В В Vain tongues from where my pictures stand apart:
В В В В Only prayer breaks the silence of the shrine
В В В В While, blackening in the daily candle-smoke,
В В В В They moulder on the damp wallпїЅs travertine,
В В В В пїЅMid echoes the light footstep never woke.
В В В В So, die my pictures! surely, gently die!
В В В В O youth, men praise so, holds their praise its worth?
В В В В Blown harshly, keeps the trump its golden cry?
В В В В Tastes sweet the water with such specks of earth?
meaning: About: Robert Browning was a British poet and playwright whose mastery of dramatic verse, especially dramatic monologues, made him one of the foremost Victorian poets.
